title: Linux Terminal Basics for Beginners
description: Learn the essential Linux terminal commands every beginner should know — navigation, files, permissions, and more.

steps.title: Open Your Terminal
steps.content: On most Linux desktops, press **Ctrl+Alt+T** to open a terminal. You'll see a prompt like `user@hostname:~$` — this is where you type commands.

steps.title: Navigate the File System
steps.content: Use `pwd` to see where you are, `ls` to list files, and `cd` to change directories. The tilde `~` is a shortcut for your home directory.

steps.title: Create and Remove Files & Folders
steps.content: Use `mkdir` to create directories, `touch` to create empty files, and `rm` to remove them.
steps.code:
mkdir my-project
touch my-project/notes.txt
rm my-project/notes.txt
rmdir my-project
steps.warning: Be very careful with rm -rf — it deletes files permanently without confirmation.

steps.title: Read and Edit Files
steps.content: Use `cat` to print file contents, `less` to scroll through large files, and `nano` for simple editing.

steps.title: Understand Permissions
steps.content: Every file has an owner, a group, and permissions. Use `ls -l` to see them and `chmod` to change them. The format is **rwx** (read, write, execute) for owner, group, and others.
steps.code:
ls -l myfile.txt
chmod 755 myscript.sh
chmod +x myscript.sh

steps.title: Install Software with Your Package Manager
steps.content: On Debian/Ubuntu, use `apt`. On Fedora, use `dnf`. On Arch, use `pacman`. Always update your package list first.
steps.code:
sudo apt update
sudo apt install htop
sudo apt remove htop

steps.title: Useful Shortcuts and Tips
steps.content:
A few things that will save you a lot of time:

- **Tab** — auto-complete file names and commands
- **Ctrl+C** — cancel the current command
- **Ctrl+R** — search command history
- **Up/Down arrows** — scroll through previous commands
- **man <command>** — read the manual for any command

seo.title: Linux Terminal Basics for Beginners | OmniSet Guide
seo.description: Learn essential Linux terminal commands. A beginner-friendly guide covering navigation, files, permissions, and package management.
